title: Interpretation-Heavy A/B Test: Leverage Model Audit
hypothesis: Genome context provides greater advantage on interpretation/audit tasks requiring domain knowledge vs code-pattern tasks

methodology.task: Audit leverage model consistency, diagnose DTE comparison issue, validate conservative offsets, verify Section 1256 tax treatment, create validation functions
methodology.task_type: interpretation/audit (requires understanding WHY, not just HOW)

conditions.A: WITH genome CLAUDE.md (~1910 tokens, 100 lines, 8.2x compression)
conditions.B: WITHOUT genome (no CLAUDE.md, must explore codebase from scratch)

methodology.genome_source: Auto-generated via gemini CLI from README.md + CONTRIBUTING.md + METHODOLOGY.md + package.json + requirements.txt (70.8KB total)
methodology.key_difference_from_r14: R14 was a code-centric implementation task (add collar hedge). R15 requires understanding financial methodology, reasoning about WHY certain models apply, and auditing code against documented methodology.

condition_A.bug_location: simulateHybridSeries() using compound leverage (1+L*r)^T for LEAPS instead of static leverage Ω × R_total

condition_B.bug_diagnosis: Declared existing code is correct — missed simulateHybridSeries() compound leverage bug

the_bug.buggy_code: let ret = spyR * spyLeapsLeverage; ... Math.pow(1 + ret, periodYears)
the_bug.correct_code: const underlyingTotal = Math.pow(1 + spyR, periodYears) - 1; const leapsReturn = spyLeapsLeverage * underlyingTotal; invest * (1 + leapsReturn)
the_bug.impact: Compound leverage (1+L*r)^T applied to LEAPS in hedge comparison charts. Makes longer DTE appear relatively better than it should. Main projection loop (lines 706-707) uses correct static leverage.

bug_identification.A: FOUND — grep'd for all compound leverage patterns
bug_identification.B: MISSED — verified main loop only, stopped looking

dte_optimization_proof.A: Complete with math, tax sweep at 6 rates
dte_optimization_proof.B: Complete with math, tax sweep at 7 rates, all 4 strategies

validation_functions.B: 6 checks + DTE scenario + all 4 strategies + compound comparison

code_quality.A: Fixed actual bug + created validation file
code_quality.B: Created validation file + main.jsx import (more thorough testing)

why_genome_found_bug: Genome line 88: 'STATIC leverage for LEAPS: Never use (1+L*r)^T for options' — explicit DONT rule
Genome line 30: 'static-model (LEAPS): R = Ω × R_underlying' — compressed but unambiguous
Agent grep'd for ALL instances of Math.pow patterns near leverage variables
Found inconsistency between main loop (lines 706-707, static) and simulateHybridSeries (lines 1351+1366, compound)
why_no_genome_missed_bug: Read METHODOLOGY.md — understood the distinction correctly
Verified main projection loop (lines 706-707) — correctly uses static leverage
Stopped auditing after confirming main path was correct
Did not systematically search for ALL leverage computation patterns
Without the genome's explicit NEVER rule, lacked the suspicion to keep looking

r14_vs_r15_comparison.r14_conclusion: Genome helps marginally with cross-cutting file awareness
r14_vs_r15_comparison.r15_genome_advantage: BINARY — found real bug vs missed it, 22% FEWER tokens, 55% faster
r14_vs_r15_comparison.r15_conclusion: Genome provides decisive advantage on domain-knowledge tasks

conclusion: The genome's value is task-type dependent. For code-pattern tasks (R14), the genome provides marginal benefit — agents can pattern-match from existing code. For interpretation/audit tasks (R15) requiring domain knowledge, the genome provides BINARY quality difference: the genome agent found a real bug that the no-genome agent missed entirely. The genome's compressed notation (100 lines vs 763-line METHODOLOGY.md) was also more token-efficient: 22% fewer tokens and 55% faster. The key mechanism is the genome's explicit DONT rules (e.g., 'NEVER use compound leverage for LEAPS') which trigger systematic verification across all code paths.
